name: Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop
name_original: Desde San Juan del Puerto a Valdere del Camino y Niebla – circular por la Vía Verde de Molinos de Agua
description: Cycle the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop, a difficult 49.3-mile route through diverse Andalusian landscapes.
region_intro: Touring cycling around San Juan Del Puerto offers diverse landscapes, from the salt marshes of Salinas del Tinto to the green areas along Arroyo Candón. The region is characterized by the estuaries of the Tinto and Odiel rivers, providing unique natural environments. Cyclists can explore routes that traverse former railway lines, coastal areas, and the varied terrain of Huelva province, which includes farmlands and natural parks. Best touring cycling routes around San Juan Del Puerto The most popular touring cycling route is Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop, a 49.3 miles (79.3 km) trail that takes 5 hours 7 minutes to complete. This difficult route connects two significant long-distance paths. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Hay Bales Near Trigueros – Vía Verde Molino de Acha loop from San Juan del Puerto, a moderate 28.7 miles (46.1 km) path. This loop starts from San Juan del Puerto and follows part of the Vía Verde. Local touring cyclists also love the Muelle de las Carabelas – Gibraleón Iron Bridge loop from Moguer, a 37.6 miles (60.5 km) trail leading through historical sites and along river estuaries, often completed in about 4 hours 30 minutes. Touring cycling around San Juan Del Puerto is defined by river estuaries, greenways (Vías Verdes), and salt marsh landscapes. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate loops to more difficult long-distance routes. The routes in San Juan Del Puerto are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 60 reviews. More than 800 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore San Juan Del Puerto's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around San Juan Del Puerto?
A: There are over 100 touring cycling routes around San Juan Del Puerto, offering a wide variety of experiences. These include 19 easy routes, 64 moderate routes, and 22 more challenging options, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while touring cycling in San Juan Del Puerto?
A: Touring cyclists in San Juan Del Puerto can explore diverse landscapes, including the unique salt marshes of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5463095" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salinas del Tinto&lt;/a&gt;, lush green areas along the Arroyo Candón with its historic Roman bridge, and the significant &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marismas del Odiel&lt;/a&gt;, home to pink flamingos. The routes also traverse coastal areas, farmlands, and former railway lines, showcasing the varied terrain of Huelva province.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, San Juan Del Puerto offers several family-friendly touring cycling routes. Many of the 19 easy routes are suitable for families, often following flat greenways or quiet paths. These routes provide a safe and enjoyable way to explore the region's natural beauty together.

Q: What historical landmarks or points of interest can I visit along the cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history and culture. Along the routes, you can encounter sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3155444" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Discovering Faith&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301135" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muelle de las Carabelas&lt;/a&gt; (Spring of the Caravels), and the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4525837" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niebla Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5463095" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muelle de Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;, an old river dock, also offers a glimpse into the area's industrial past.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes starting from San Juan Del Puerto?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. For example, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31561469" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hay Bales Near Trigueros – Vía Verde Molino de Acha loop from San Juan del Puerto&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option that follows part of a Vía Verde. Another moderate choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31561534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sculpture of a Man with a Bull – Huelva–Gibraleón Cycle Path loop from San Juan del Puerto&lt;/a&gt;, offering a scenic ride.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in San Juan Del Puerto?
A: San Juan Del Puerto benefits from a mild climate and abundant sunshine, making it suitable for touring cycling throughout much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for longer rides, while the mild winters can also be pleasant for cycling.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about the routes in San Juan Del Puerto?
A: The touring cycling routes in San Juan Del Puerto are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 60 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the well-integrated greenways (Vías Verdes), and the unique natural environments found along the river estuaries and salt marshes.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling options available?
A: Yes, the region connects to significant long-distance paths. The difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984090915/via-verde-de-los-molinos-del-agua-and-transandalus-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, combines two major routes for an extensive touring experience. The EuroVelo 1 (Atlantic Coast Route) also passes near San Juan del Puerto, offering maritime-themed journeys.

Q: Can I find routes that follow old railway lines?
A: Absolutely. The region is known for its greenways, or Vías Verdes, which are often developed along disused railway lines. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31561469" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hay Bales Near Trigueros – Vía Verde Molino de Acha loop from San Juan del Puerto&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that utilizes these historic paths, providing flat and scenic cycling opportunities.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Tinto and Odiel rivers?
A: Many routes in the San Juan Del Puerto area are defined by the estuaries of the Tinto and Odiel rivers. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38261504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muelle de las Carabelas – Gibraleón Iron Bridge loop from Moguer&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, leads along these river estuaries, offering picturesque views and opportunities to observe the unique estuarine flora and fauna.

Q: Is public transport accessible near the cycling routes?
A: While specific public transport details for each route vary, San Juan del Puerto itself has connections that can facilitate access to the starting points of some routes. The town's old train station, for example, is a known starting point for trails like the "Senderos de las Salinas."

Q: Are there any routes that pass through historic towns or villages?
A: Yes, several routes allow you to explore charming towns and villages. The circular route from San Juan del Puerto, for example, can lead you through Moguer, known for its wines and literary history, and Palos de la Frontera, a historically significant "Columbian place" with sites like La Rábida monastery.
tips: This 33 km greenway between San Juan del Puerto and Valverde del Camino passes through an old mining train, from the banks of El Tinto to the ravines of the Serranía de Huelva. Its name comes from the ethnographic set of the Water Mills in Beas - these are flour mills from the 17th, 18th and 19th centuries.
Alcázar o Castillo de los Guzmánes has some remnants of Roman construction, its 2000 years-old concrete mix survives better than contemporary concrete
Big square in the historic town center
Medieval southeastern city gate with mudejar elements
Actually a busy road between San Juan del Puerto and Niebla, but thanks to the hard shoulder it is good to cycle.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the towns connected by the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua, such as San Juan del Puerto or Valverde del Camino. These towns serve as excellent starting points for your cycling adventure.

faqs.question: Is the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua section is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash and ensure you clean up after them to maintain the trail's cleanliness for everyone.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best seasons for cycling the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua and Transandalus Loop are autumn and spring. Summers can be very hot in Andalusia, making long rides challenging, and higher altitudes may experience snow in winter.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to access the trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or have to pay an entrance fee to cycle the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua or the Transandalus Loop. Both are publicly accessible routes.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua section?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua features a mixed surface, primarily compacted earth and asphalt, making it generally smooth for cycling. You'll pass through diverse landscapes including olive groves, sunflower fields, and pine forests.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is recommended for the Transandalus Loop section?
faqs.answer: For the Transandalus Loop, a full-suspension mountain bike with wide tires and good tread is highly recommended. This section involves hard gravel, dirt tracks, and potentially unpaved segments with loose sand, streams, and stones.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a blend of natural beauty and historical sites. Along the way, you can explore the Greenway of the Water Mills, visit Niebla Castle, and discover historical sites like San Martín Square and Puerta del Buey.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates significant sections of other official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Transandalus, the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua, and the Camino de Santiago del Sur, among others.

faqs.question: Are there any specific wildlife or bird species I might encounter?
faqs.answer: On the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua section, you might spot various bird species such as Crested Larks, Black Kites, Montague's Harriers, and Common Buzzards, especially in the diverse landscapes of olive groves and pine forests.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua section, with its few slopes and smooth surface, is generally suitable for a wide range of cyclists, including families and those with reduced mobility. However, the overall 'difficult' rating for the combined loop indicates that the Transandalus sections require good fitness and advanced riding skills, making the full loop less ideal for beginners.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, water, or accommodation along the way?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua connects towns like San Juan del Puerto and Valverde del Camino, where you can find amenities. The Transandalus Loop is designed with stages ending in towns that offer sufficient facilities for overnight stays and other services, so planning your stops in these towns is advisable.

waypoints.name: Road between San Juan del Puerto and Niebla
